notes: Seventh validated clue batch compiled from official school websites.
Clues intentionally avoid revealing enrollment size, grade span, or directional/proximity information because those are gameplay feedback channels.

clues.text: Its mission is expressed in three words: All Children Thrive.
Official history says it was the state's first Project Based Learning public school and the first diverse-by-design public charter school in the Southern United States.
The school says it nurtures children's natural eagerness through child-centered Project Based Learning, arts integration, and outdoor learning.
Its published values for children include kindness, empathy, generosity, honesty, and learning through play, art, music, dance, drama, and creative writing.
The executive director's message says the school was one of the state's first 100 charter schools.
Official pages list Arlie Harris as Executive Director and Chanel Carrell as board chair.
Its mission says the school fosters a rich learning environment that embodies the essence of community, understanding, and the human spirit.
Official materials say students learn by doing through meaningful projects, hands-on experiences, and strong academic instruction.

The school organizes its approach around four "FernLeaf Fundamentals": Rich Learning Environments, Community and Culture, Social and Emotional Growth and Development, and High Quality Academic Instruction.
Its fundamentals page says every student participates in at least eight fieldwork experiences each year, with overnight experiences built into the program.
The executive director wrote that he is the founder of the school.
The team page lists Michael Luplow as Executive Director, with Jenn Love and Molly Luplow serving as Lower School and Upper School Directors.
Its mission says the school inspires students to enjoy and lead their own learning.
Official history says the school opened its doors in 2002.
The school says its students are engaged in an experientially rich, hands-on course of study designed to maximize each child's potential as a responsible citizen of local and global communities.
Its sports program is described as being based on the Outward Bound principle of Collaboration and Competition.
The school has an afterschool program called JAM that encourages the growth of young musicians.
Official staff pages say the school is led by three Co-Directors: Crim Bassett, Billie Clemens, and Tracy Wharton.
Its homepage quotes Benjamin Franklin: "Tell me and I forget. Teach me and I remember. Involve me and I learn."
The school says innovation is not a passive process and that students are challenged with big questions and problems so they learn to lead.
Its story says the school grew out of two questions about why school has not changed enough and what skills students need in a rapidly changing world.
Official history says the school joined a national network of EL Education schools and opened in 2014.
Its website says community engagement is at the core of the school, citing projects such as cleaning up Hominy Creek, planting trees, and partnering with Habitat for Humanity.
Official pages list Michelle Vruwink as Executive Director and Al Glicksberg as board chair.
Its mission says the school supports each student's continuing discovery and development of self and community through a learning environment that is relevant, active, and project oriented.
The school's published core principles include Inquisitiveness, Environmental Responsibility, Active Learning, Empathy, Mindfulness, Innovation, Resilience, and Connection to Community.
Its homepage says the school is founded on the Experiential Learning framework and uses the community as a vital resource.
The curriculum page says every 8th grader completes a Passage Portfolio capstone with a public presentation.
Official history says the school began serving families in the 2005-2006 school year and later earned NC Green School of Quality recognition.
The board page lists Jennifer Kirby as board chair and Natalie Oransky as director.
